nativeRange.habitat: Intertidal and shallow subtidal rocky and sandy shores; estuaries, tide pools, and brackish coastal waters; tolerant of wide salinity fluctuations

imageCandidates.notes: Magnefl; 3264x2448. Sharp, vibrant grass-green single inflated frond on rock clearly showing the diagnostic hollow, intestine-like tubular form; minor other algae in top-left corner.
W.carter; 4717x3145. Striking, tack-sharp vibrant green tuft isolated on orange rock; excellent color contrast, single subject, filaments read slightly hair-like.
Magnefl; 3435x1702. Sharp isolated translucent tubular fronds in a water basin; clean single subject clearly showing the hollow tube structure.

summary: Gutweed is a fast-growing green macroalgae recognizable by its hollow, tubular fronds. Found naturally in estuaries and tide pools worldwide, it is well suited to refugium use, where it absorbs nitrate and phosphate as it grows. Beginner-friendly and tolerant of a wide salinity range.
careNotes:
Gutweed is the tubular-frond member of the Ulva genus, distinguished from its close relative [clean-sea-lettuce] (Ulva lactuca) by its hollow, intestine-like cylindrical fronds rather than a flat, sheet-like thallus. Fronds typically reach 10–30 cm in length and 6–18 mm in width, with rounded tips and irregularly arranged cells.

The species was formerly classified as Enteromorpha intestinalis and placed in the genus Enteromorpha. Genetic work in the early 2000s demonstrated that the tubular and flat Ulva and Enteromorpha forms are not phylogenetically distinct — that is, the two groups are too closely related to warrant separate genera — and the species was reclassified as Ulva intestinalis. The older name Enteromorpha intestinalis persists in much of the aquarium hobby literature.

Moderate flow is generally advised.

Gutweed is naturally cosmopolitan and highly euryhaline (tolerant of a wide range of salinities). Wild populations are commonly documented in estuaries, tide pools, and brackish coastal waters spanning salinity ranges from near-freshwater to full marine. For refugium use, standard reef parameters (SG 1.023–1.026, 24–26°C, pH 8.0–8.4) are appropriate, though the species has been reported tolerating SG as low as 1.005 in its natural estuarine habitat.

Growth rate is generally fast, consistent with other Ulva species. As a nutrient exporter it is regarded as effective at assimilating nitrate and phosphate, and regular harvesting is advised to remove the accumulated nutrients from the system. Gutweed is less commonly offered in the hobby than sea lettuce (U. lactuca) or Chaetomorpha, and is available from a small number of specialty suppliers; water parameters and husbandry guidance are extrapolated from genus-level Ulva data and wild ecological records.
breedingNotes: Gutweed propagates readily by fragmentation in the aquarium — detached frond segments will regrow under suitable conditions. The species also undergoes sexual reproduction through an alternation of generations life cycle: it produces two physically similar free-living stages, one that generates spores and one that generates gametes, which alternate across the organism's lifetime. This is characteristic of the broader Ulva life cycle. Deliberate management of this reproductive cycle in captivity is not documented.

primary.name: Mosaic Macros
primary.notes: Sole aquarium hobby source. Confirms scientific name Ulva intestinalis, common name Gutweed, tubular/hair-like filament form, refugium placement, nutrient-export function. Specific water parameters not provided on product page.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Ulva intestinalis
additional.notes: Confirmed taxonomy (Ulvaceae / Ulvales), synonym Enteromorpha intestinalis, reclassification via early-2000s genetic work, cosmopolitan distribution, tubular frond morphology (10–30 cm, 6–18 mm wide), brackish/estuarine habitat, isomorphic alternation of generations life cycle. Used as basis for size range, native range, and salinity tolerance.

additional.name: AlgaeBarn — Clean Sea Lettuce (Ulva lactuca sibling reference)
additional.notes: Cross-reference for Ulva genus husbandry: confirms fast growth, refugium placement, high nutrient uptake, herbivore food value, shallow rocky reef habitat. Water parameters for U. intestinalis inferred at genus level — brackish tolerance differentiated from U. lactuca based on Wikipedia ecological data.

additional.name: Mosaic Macros general macroalgae care guide
additional.notes: Slice-wide secondary source — provides authoritative Mosaic-wide parameter targets (temp 22-26°C, pH 8.1-8.4, Ca 400-450 ppm, Mg 1250-1350 ppm, NO3 5-20 ppm, PO4 0.02-0.1 ppm) and the 4-method propagation standard. Added retroactively 2026-06-08.
